title: 〜にくい
description: 〜にくい is a verb suffix that means "difficult to do (the verb)."

content.text: Derived from the obsolete adjective 難（かた）い（にく ） (difficult), 〜にくい is used to describe the difficulty of an action or achievement, in much the same way that we might say "difficult to do something" in English. To express the opposite, the ease of doing something, we have 〜やすい , which is derived from the adjective 易（やす）い（やす ） (easy).

〜にくい expresses the difficulty of doing something as a characteristic of whatever you're talking about, rather than a characteristic of the person who is doing the action. For example, when you say "このフォークは使（つか）いにくい," it suggests "This fork is difficult to use (because it has a weird shape)," but not "This fork is difficult to use (because I am terrible at using forks)."
にくい for Physical and Psychological Difficulty
〜にくい can be used for both physical and psychological difficulty. First, let's take a look at an example of physical difficulty:

content.japanese: このフォークは使（つか）いにくい。
content.english: This fork is difficult to use.

content.text: Maybe the fork is weirdly shaped or is made of soft material that makes it difficult for you to use. Now let's look at an example that shows psychological difficulty:

content.japanese: このフォークは使（つか）いにくい。
content.english: This fork is difficult to use.

content.text: Ha! These two examples look… and they are identical. Because the sentences didn't come with any context, how we interpret them is totally up to us. So what could be the psychological difficulty of using this fork? Maybe earlier you saw the fork being licked by your mom (eww!), or maybe it's the fork you and your ex picked out together and has so much emotional value that just looking at it makes you cry (aww).
にくい for Tolerance and Unlikeliness

Since 〜にくい states the difficulty as an attribute of whatever we're talking about, it can be used to describe someone's tolerance, or the unlikeliness that someone will do something, often related to something innate about that person. When 〜にくい is used this way, the English translation is usually something like "Y doesn't X easily." Let's take a look at an example:

content.japanese: 私（わたし）の姉（あね）は車（くるま）酔（よ）いしにくい。
content.english: My older sister doesn't get car sick easily.

content.text: This would literally translate to "My older sister is difficult to get car sick," indicating that the person's sister has tolerance against getting car sick.

Again, the main mission of 〜にくい is to describe an attribute of whatever you're talking about. Since it was originally derived from the adjective 難（かた）い（にく ）, this form also behaves like an adjective. Therefore, oftentimes, you will see this 〜にくい modifying nouns just like adjectives do (and it is useful to be able to use it in this way!).
For example, you can say things like:

content.japanese: 使（つか）いにくいフォーク
content.english: a fork that's difficult to use

content.japanese: 歩（ある）きにくい靴（くつ）
content.english: a pair of shoes that are difficult to walk with

content.text: Earlier, I mentioned 〜にくい came from the obsolete adjective 難（かた）い（にく ） (difficult). As you may know, the same kanji is used for a very common adjective that means "difficult" — むずかしい. There is yet another way of reading 難（かた）い, which is がたい. Just like 〜にくい, 〜がたい can be paired up with a verb to show the difficulty of doing something. However, 〜がたい is often used in writing and has a stiff feeling to it. It is also only used with certain non-physical action verbs as idiomatic expressions. For example, paired up with 信（しん）じる (to believe), you can say:

content.japanese: そのニュースは信（しん）じがたい。
content.english: The news is difficult to believe.
